agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Mechanism of Action
agent1_snippets.content: Meta-analysis indicated that for preventing heavy drinking, NNT (number needed to treat) is 12 (95% CI, -0.13 to -0.04) with oral naltrexone. [10] In a clinical trial, extended-release naltrexone had a higher rate of opioid-negative urine samples (74% vs 56%, P<0.001) at week 24 compared to counseling and participation in community programs. According to guidelines by the American Society of Addiction Medicine (ASAM), a combination of buprenorphine and low doses of oral naltrexone is effective for opioid use disorder for managing withdrawal. [11] [12]

agent1_snippets.title: Addiction Relapse Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Medications
agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone is a medication used to help prevent relapses on alcohol by reducing cravings. It is available in an oral tablet and a monthly injection. Studies have shown that the use of naltrexone is associated with a reduced risk of relapse with a number-needed-to-treat (NNT) to prevent a return to any drinking of 20. [14] Acamprosate is another medication used to help prevent relapses of alcohol use. Studies have shown an NNT to prevent a return to any drinking of 12. [14] Opioids

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone is FDA-approved for alcohol use disorder. [1] Naltrexone is also FDA-approved for opioid use disorder. [2] A fixed-dose combination of naltrexone and bupropion is FDA-approved for obesity. [3] Off-label use includes treating cholestatic pruritus in adults. [4] Researchers are studying its use in patients with stimulant use disorder, particularly patients with polydrug dependence on opioids, heroin, and amphetamine. The drug is part of the current best practices for addressing and treating patients with opioid use disorder. [5]

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Administration -- Note
agent1_snippets.content: The patient first needs detoxification from opioids before starting naltrexone; the patient must be opioid-free (including tramadol) for at least 7 to 10 days. Advise patients not to self-administer opioids while receiving naltrexone therapy. Since there is a possibility of opioid withdrawal, the recommended approach is to try a naloxone challenge to confirm the appropriateness of the treatment plan.

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Administration -- Opioid Use Disorder
agent1_snippets.content: For opioid use disorder, naltrexone can be started at a lower dose of 25 mg once daily for a few days and then increased to 50 mg once daily, 100 mg every other day, 150 mg every 3rd day, or 380 mg injection depot form for monthly administration. According to ASAM guidelines, extended-release injectable naltrexone 380 mg should be administered by deep IM injection in the gluteal muscle every four weeks. [11] Naltrexone is typically combined with an alpha-2 agonist, which minimizes patient discomfort during withdrawal and shortens the duration of opioid withdrawal. Another benefit of rapid opiate detoxification is the reduced time between opioid use and the initiation of sustained naltrexone treatment to prevent relapse. Rapid opiate detoxification completion rates using naltrexone and clonidine range from 75% to 81% compared to 40% for methadone and clonidine alone.

agent1_snippets.content: Alcohol: As many as 70% of individuals relapse after psychosocial treatment alone Naltrexone in toxicity and Supportive care. Disulfiram should only be used in patients who are highly motivated to maintain abstinence. Treat Wernicke-Korsakoff with vitamin B-1 (thiamine) intravenously.

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Administration -- Oral Dosage
agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone is available as an oral tablet (50 mg), and the usual dose for alcohol treatment is 50 to 100 mg. Dosing should start at 25 mg orally for a single dose and be repeated in an hour if there are no withdrawal signs. Naltrexone can be given orally with or without food. Administration with or after meals may minimize adverse gastrointestinal (GI) effects.

agent1_snippets.title: Opioid Antagonists -- Adverse Effects
agent1_snippets.content: Accelerated protocols for medically supervised management of withdrawal aim to reduce the transition time to naltrexone treatment, thereby reducing patient inconvenience, lengths of inpatient or residential stay, and treatment costs. This method has not found widespread use. During a supervised naltrexone-accelerated withdrawal, a “naloxone challenge” can be performed to ensure a complete system depletion of exogenous opioids prior to full-dose oral naltrexone or extended-release intramuscular naltrexone (Vivitrol). A low naloxone starting dose of just 0.1 mg intravenous is given, and the patient is monitored for up to 30 minutes for signs of withdrawal. Typically, symptoms present within 1-5 minutes. A doubled dose of 0.2 mg follows, and the patient undergoes observation. Thirty minutes following, a final dose of 0.4 mg is given, and if the patient is asymptomatic, the assumption is that the patient can safely receive naltrexone without fear of withdrawal symptoms. The extended-release injectable version of naltrexone has found increased favor among practitioners for opiate maintenance treatment as it only needs dosing every four weeks. [11]

agent1_snippets.title: Opioid Use Disorder: Evaluation and Management -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone may also be used in patients with OUD. Naltrexone works by blocking opioid effects and helps maintain abstinence from opioids by antagonizing the mu-opioid receptor. Naltrexone may only be initiated when the patient is free of physiological opioid dependence, and at least seven days without acute withdrawal symptoms are required before starting the medication. Both oral and intramuscular naltrexone are superior to placebo in maintaining abstinence from opioids, but the intramuscular form may be more effective. [34] The intramuscular form may also offer better compliance due to monthly administrations. Intramuscular naltrexone is FDA-approved for opioid dependence, and naltrexone administration following completion of treatment with buprenorphine has shown to be an effective treatment in OUD. [35] [36]

Practitioners must still comply with applicable state limits regarding treating patients with OUD. agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Adverse Effects -- Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act
agent1_snippets.content: As of December 2022, the MAT Act has eliminated the DATA-Waiver (X-Waiver) program. All DEA-registered practitioners with Schedule III authority may now prescribe buprenorphine for OUD in their practice if applicable state law permits, and SAMHSA encourages them to do so. Prescribers registered as DATA-Waiver prescribers will receive a new DEA registration certificate reflecting this change; no action is needed from registrants.

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Indications
agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone, an opioid antagonist used to treat alcohol use disorder and opioid dependence, was developed in 1963 and patented in 1967. In 1984, naltrexone received approval for medical use in the United States. While experimenting with rats at the University of Pennsylvania, Dr. Joseph Volpicelli first recognized naltrexone's potential to treat alcohol use disorder. The World Health Organization (WHO) estimates that over 75 million people have alcohol use disorder or dependence worldwide. Naltrexone is a mu-opioid receptor antagonist.

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
agent1_snippets.content: Clinical opiate withdrawal scale (COWS) helps to rate common signs/symptoms of opiate withdrawal and monitor symptoms over time. The naltrexone readiness form is used to verify the patient's medical history, drug use history, lab information, physical exam findings, and other pertinent information to check patient readiness. The naltrexone education form ensures the patient understands how naltrexone works, the length of treatment, and the adverse effects. Initial and follow-up medication management forms help patients track and maintain abstinence.

agent1_snippets.title: Opioid Toxicity(Archived) -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Chronic treatment of patients with opioid use disorder using methadone (a full mu receptor agonist), buprenorphine (a partial agonist of mu receptors and Kappa antagonist), and naltrexone (an opioid receptor antagonist) became more accepted. Increasing numbers of studies comparing various strategies of recovery and relapse suppression appeared in the literature. Concurrently, depot naltrexone injection to enhance complete opioid avoidance became available. Each naltrexone injection lasts approximately 30 days. During that time, opioids are rendered ineffective by the effect of naltrexone on the target receptors. [22] [23]

agent1_snippets.title: Drug Addiction -- Treatment / Management
agent1_snippets.content: Pharmacologically, alcohol dependence is treatable with disulfiram, naltrexone, and acamprosate. [33] Each has its place in alcohol dependence and addiction. Disulfiram is effective in managing a patient who recently quit and needs help maintaining abstinence, as any consumed alcohol causes relatively quick hangover-type symptoms to deter further drinking. Naltrexone removes the sensation of reward or pleasure with drinking, and acamprosate minimizes the initial withdrawal symptoms. Alcohol intoxication during episodes of relapse is treatable with long-acting benzodiazepines such as chlordiazepoxide or diazepam. [34]

agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone blocks the effect of opioids and prevents opioid intoxication and physiologic dependence on opioid users. Naltrexone also modifies the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis to suppress ethanol consumption.

agent1_snippets.title: Disulfiram -- Indications -- Food and Drug Administration-Approved Indication
agent1_snippets.content: It is a second-line option (acamprosate and naltrexone are first-line treatments) in patients with sufficient physician supervision. Disulfiram is safe and efficient in supervised short-term and long-term treatment of individuals dependent on alcohol but who are motivated to discontinue alcohol use. [1] The American Psychiatric Association (APA) 2018 guidelines endorse the use of disulfiram for alcohol use disorder. According to APA recommendations, disulfiram should be considered for patients exhibiting moderate to severe alcohol use disorder under specific conditions. This includes individuals with a primary goal of achieving abstinence and those who express a preference for disulfiram or demonstrate intolerance or lack of response to naltrexone and acamprosate. Furthermore, candidates for disulfiram treatment should possess the capability to comprehend the risks associated with alcohol consumption while taking the medication, and they should have no contraindications to its use. [2]

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Adverse Effects -- Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act
agent1_snippets.content: The Mainstreaming Addiction Treatment (MAT) Act provision updates federal guidelines to expand the availability of evidence-based treatment to address the opioid epidemic. The MAT Act empowers all health care providers with a standard controlled substance license to prescribe buprenorphine for opioid use disorder (OUD), just as they prescribe other essential medications. The MAT Act is intended to help destigmatize a standard of care for OUD and integrate substance use disorder treatment across healthcare settings.

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Administration -- Injection Dosage
agent1_snippets.content: Naltrexone is also available in a depot injection (380 mg). The IM form requires injection using provided needles for administration into the upper outer quadrant of the gluteal area; clinicians should avoid injection into the blood vessel. [14] The drug should not be administered intravenously (IV), subcutaneously, or into fatty tissue. The IM injection is recommended because many patients with a substance use disorder are non-compliant with the oral formula. [15] Data indicate that outcomes for patients with alcohol dependence who take naltrexone may be more favorable when using the IM formulation.

agent1_snippets.title: Naltrexone -- Administration -- Specific Patient Populations
agent1_snippets.content: Pregnancy Considerations: Naltrexone is classified as category C for use during pregnancy, which means that the risk to the fetus of adverse effects cannot be ruled out. There is limited research available related to the use of naltrexone during pregnancy. Animal reproduction studies have shown adverse effects. If a female treated with naltrexone to treat opioid use disorder becomes pregnant, naltrexone therapy should be stopped if the mother and the physician agree that the risk of relapse is low. According to ACOG, the decision regarding naltrexone treatment during pregnancy requires a risk-benefit analysis considering the possible risk of relapse with naltrexone discontinuation. Methadone or buprenorphine is usually preferred during pregnancy for opioid use disorder. [17]

agent1_snippets.title: Addiction Relapse Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Medications
agent1_snippets.content: A wide variety of medications targeting diverse pathways have been studied for methamphetamine addiction. Agents studied include antidepressants, psychostimulants, topiramate, baclofen, gabapentin, antipsychotics, N-acetylcysteine, acamprosate, oxazepam, naltrexone, atomoxetine, oxytocin, and others. However, no agents have shown clear efficacy in reducing the risk of relapse. In light of many negative studies, researchers have called for the exploration of novel agents. As a result, it is likely that effective medication treatment for methamphetamine addiction will not be available soon. [18]

agent1_snippets.title: Addiction Relapse Prevention -- Clinical Significance -- Medications
agent1_snippets.content: Disulfiram is a medication that inhibits aldehyde dehydrogenase resulting in the build-up of acetaldehyde, which produces uncomfortable physical effects. As a result, disulfiram acts as a deterrent against an alcohol relapse until the body metabolizes the medications. One significant challenge regarding the use of disulfiram is non-adherence. Supervised treatment with disulfiram has correlated with an increased time to relapse and a reduced number of drinking days. [13] Disulfiram has been shown superior to naltrexone and acamprosate but only when used in observed dosing. Such results are unlikely outside of observed therapy due to frequent discontinuation.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2385
agent2_snippets.content: Alcohol Deterrents/Anti-craving agents are classified as a subcategory of naltrexone hydrochloride, and naltrexone hydrochloride is itself placed within the broader category of الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية (dependence on opioid compounds). This hierarchy indicates that naltrexone hydrochloride is a medication primarily associated with the management of opioid dependence, and that formulations used to deter alcohol consumption or reduce craving are considered specific applications of this drug. Recognizing these relationships helps clinicians appreciate the shared pharmacologic basis between treatments for alcohol craving and opioid dependence, supporting more integrated and informed approaches to managing substance‑use disorders.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1445
agent2_snippets.content: Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving is classified as a specific application of naltrexone hydrochloride, indicating that naltrexone hydrochloride serves as the parent compound for this anti‑craving agent. Both Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving and the condition الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية (opioid dependence) are encompassed within the broader therapeutic class Anti‑Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents. This hierarchy shows that naltrexone hydrochloride, a medication used to treat الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية, also underlies the alcohol‑deterrent formulation, linking the treatment of opioid dependence and alcohol craving under a common anti‑addiction pharmacologic framework. Managing patients with either alcohol craving or opioid dependence may therefore involve agents that belong to the Anti‑Addiction/ Substance Abuse Treatment Agents category, with naltrexone hydrochloride playing a central role.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
agent2_snippets.content: Centrally acting antiobesity products constitute a pharmacologic class that promotes weight loss by influencing central nervous system pathways involved in appetite and energy balance. This class includes the combination therapy bupropion / naltrexone, the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide, and the serotonin‑norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor sibutramine. Bupropion / naltrexone works by modulating reward and satiety circuits, setmelanotide targets genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin signaling, and sibutramine reduces appetite by increasing synaptic concentrations of serotonin and norepinephrine. Together, these centrally acting agents illustrate the diverse mechanisms by which central pathways can be leveraged to treat obesity, highlighting the importance of individualized therapeutic selection based on efficacy, safety profile, and underlying patient characteristics.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 1 -- Community 126
agent2_snippets.content: Centrally acting antiobesity preparations, such as the bupropion/naltrexone combination and sibutramine, belong to the antiobesity product class alongside peripherally acting agents. The bupropion/naltrexone formulation incorporates the opioid antagonist naltrexone, which is also classified among drugs used in alcohol dependence and serves as an alcohol‑withdrawal agent. Naltrexone and disulfiram are members of the same therapeutic class for alcohol dependence; disulfiram can prevent relapse but is contraindicated in patients receiving paraldehyde. Citalopram, an SSRI antidepressant, may be employed in the management of alcohol dependence, illustrating the overlap between antidepressant therapy and substance‑related disorders. Within the taxonomy of substance‑related and addictive disorders (الاضطرابات المتعلقة بالمواد والإدمان), subcategories include substance use, alcohol dependence (الاعتماد على الكحول), and drug‑use disorder, with antidepressant abuse (misbruik; antidepressiva) defined as a specific type of drug‑use disorder. Alcohol intoxication (التسمم الكحولي) is classified as a child entity of this broader disorder group, emphasizing its role in systematic assessment, treatment planning, and epidemiological monitoring. Poisoning, particularly alcoholic poisoning, is linked to drug‑induced lipolysis poisoning and adrenal syndrome, and is associated with alcohol dependence, underscoring the risk of toxic outcomes in this population. These relationships demonstrate how antiobesity agents, antidepressants, and alcohol‑dependence medications intersect within therapeutic and adverse‑effect frameworks, and how various forms of poisoning and substance‑use disorders are organized within a comprehensive medical taxonomy.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
agent2_snippets.content: Antidota, edrofonium, and naltrexon are interrelated pharmacologic categories. Antidota is classified as a type of naltrexon, indicating that agents within the antidota group share the opioid‑antagonist properties of naltrexon. Edrofonium is an inverse_isa of antidota, meaning that edrofonium is a specific medication that belongs to the broader antidota class. Consequently, edrofonium inherits the characteristics of both the antidota category and the naltrexon family, making it effectively a naltrexon‑type antidote. This hierarchical relationship clarifies the positioning of edrofonium within opioid‑antagonist therapy and can inform clinical decisions related to overdose management and related patient care.

agent2_snippets.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 415
agent2_snippets.content: Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving is classified as a child of naltrexone hydrochloride, indicating that naltrexone hydrochloride serves as the parent compound for this anti‑craving agent. The condition described by the Arabic term الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية (“dependence on opiate compounds”) is also linked to naltrexone hydrochloride as a child entity, reflecting the drug’s role in treating opiate dependence. Both Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving and الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية are organized under the broader therapeutic class Anti‑Addiction/Substance Abuse Treatment Agents, which groups interventions aimed at reducing addiction to alcohol and opiates. Anti‑Addiction/Substance Abuse Treatment Agents is itself placed within the USP Medicare Model Guidelines, signifying that this entire class of anti‑addiction therapies is recognized and potentially covered under Medicare’s guideline framework. In the same hierarchical structure, Hormonal Agents, Stimulant/Replacement/Modifying (Sex Hormones/Modifiers) also has the USP Medicare Model Guidelines as its parent, linking hormonal therapy categories to the same Medicare guideline system. This hierarchy—from specific agents and conditions (naltrexone hydrochloride, Alcohol Deterrents/Anti‑craving, الاعتماد على المركبات الأفيونية) through the anti‑addiction therapeutic class to the overarching USP Medicare Model Guidelines—provides a clear framework for formulary management, coverage decisions, and integrated patient care across addiction and hormonal treatment domains.
